content: Blockchain is a new way to record transactions that doesn’t rely on a central bank or single authority. Instead, it uses a network of many independent computers, each keeping a copy of the transaction record. When a transaction occurs, it’s grouped with others into a “block” and sent to these computers for verification. They use complex math to confirm the transactions are valid, then add the block to their records. Because every computer updates simultaneously, it’s almost impossible to cheat or alter past transactions without everyone noticing. This shared, synchronized record is called a “distributed ledger,” and it tracks all transactions openly. Without a central bank, blockchain can reduce costs and speed up transactions, especially across borders. In short, blockchain creates trust through many computers working together, making financial records secure and transparent without a middleman.
